hint.first_move: Use the buttons below to move between locations. Each move costs stamina and time.
hint.first_examine: Examine objects to gain Insight — your currency for unlocking deeper truths.
hint.first_npc: Talk to islanders to build trust. Higher trust unlocks secrets they won't share with a stranger.
hint.first_insight: You've gathered Insight. Visit the Archive Desk to bank it — banked Insight persists across death.
hint.first_journal: Your journal tracks active quests and field notes. Quest steps update as you progress.
hint.night_warning: Dusk is falling. Return to the lighthouse to light the beam — or face what stirs in the dark.

game.title: Echoes of the Lighthouse
game.tagline: Every loop reveals more. Every choice persists.

journal.title: The Journal
journal.no_threads_active: No threads in progress yet. Seal insights to reveal chains.

death.title: The loop resets.
death.night_danger: Consumed by the dark between the loops.
death.stamina_depleted: Your body gave out before the truth could.

keepers_cottage.name: Keeper's Cottage
keepers_cottage.desc: A modest dwelling near the cliff path. The smell of old paper and sea salt. The previous keeper lived and worked here.

lighthouse_base.name: Lighthouse
lighthouse_base.desc: The base of the great tower. Its shadow falls long across the rocks. The mechanism hums faintly.

lighthouse_top.name: Lighthouse — Lantern Room
lighthouse_top.desc: The lens room. From here you can see the whole island — and something in the water below.

mechanism_room.name: Mechanism Room
mechanism_room.desc: Gears the size of millstones turn without apparent power. The hum from above is louder here. The walls are covered in symbols no one on the island claims to recognise.

village_square.name: Village Square
village_square.desc: The heart of the island. Villagers move with purpose, eyes cast low. The well stands dry.

harbor.name: The Harbour
harbor.desc: Weathered boats creak against the dock. Silas watches the horizon. The fog is thick this morning.

chapel.name: St. Maret's Chapel
chapel.desc: The pews are empty but the candles are lit. Someone tends this place. The stained glass is cracked; the saint's face replaced with rough plaster.

mill.name: The Old Mill
mill.desc: The wheel still turns though the stream has been dry for years. The miller's log is open on the table, the last entry three weeks ago.

forest_path.name: Forest Path
forest_path.desc: The trees press close. Roots cross the path like sleeping fingers. You hear movement that stops when you stop.

ruins.name: The Old Ruins
ruins.desc: Crumbled walls of what was once a manor. The east wing still stands, barely. The smell of old smoke that will not quite leave.

cliffside.name: Cliffside
cliffside.desc: Wind tears at your coat. The cliff drops eighty feet to the rocks below. You can see the lighthouse from here — and the shape beneath the water.

archive_basement.name: Archive Basement
archive_basement.desc: Stone steps descending into cold air. Rows of iron shelving. Maren's domain. Water stains mark the walls at hip height — the flood came this far, once.

tidal_caves.name: Tidal Caves
tidal_caves.desc: Seawater pools on the cave floor, lit from below by something bioluminescent. The tide is coming in. You have perhaps an hour.

error.save_failed: Save failed. Progress may be lost.

liberation.title: Liberation
liberation.text: The Vael dissolves into light, carrying secrets older than the island itself. You stand at the lighthouse top as the mechanism goes quiet. The sea is calm. You are free.
liberation.subtitle: Some chains do not break. They dissolve — when what they held has finally been fully seen.
liberation.opening:
The seventh insight coheres in your mind like a lock turning. You are standing in the mechanism room, your hands against the central housing, and the signal sequence you have learned is no longer something you perform — it is something you become. The gears do not grind. They simply stop.

The lighthouse beam sweeps once more across the water, slower than you have ever seen it, and as it passes over the deep channel beneath the harbour, something vast and luminous rises to meet it. Not breaking the surface. Simply rising close enough to be known. The Vael. The thing that has been bound here since before the island had its name. It rises toward the light it was always part of, and the light opens to receive it.

The mechanism exhales. That is the only word for what happens — a long, structural release, like a building settling after a storm. The hum that you have lived inside for so many loops drops out of existence. The air in the room is suddenly just air. Cold, damp, smelling of stone and tide. You breathe it as though for the first time.

On the island above, windows open. Dogs stop barking at nothing. Elara, at the water's edge, watches something leave the surface and does not look away until it has entirely gone. The loop does not reset. Morning comes, and it is simply morning. It continues, without revision, into afternoon, and then evening, and then night. The lighthouse burns because you lit it. Not because anything is owed.

epilogue.maren: She reopens the archive and spends three weeks attempting to reconstruct what she burned. The gaps are permanent. She fills them with a notation she has never used before: unknown — destroyed in fear. It is, she tells no one, the most honest thing she has ever written.
epilogue.silas: The ferry returns on a Tuesday, as it always claimed it would. Silas stands on the dock and does not wave. He checks the manifest twice. Every name on it is real.
epilogue.petra: Her family's memory-suppressing medicines cease to work entirely — she discovers this when a patient reports remembering everything they had asked her to make them forget. She considers this a problem for approximately four days before deciding it is not.
epilogue.tobias: The replacement gears he made for the mechanism begin to corrode without the mechanism's energy to preserve them. He does not replace them. When they finally seize, he dismantles them carefully, as if handling something that deserves attention even in failure.
epilogue.elara: She watches the deep channel each dawn for seventeen mornings after the liberation. The bioluminescence does not return. On the eighteenth morning she cries, briefly and without understanding exactly why, and then goes back to work.

liberation.closing: The island is still an island. The lighthouse still stands. The mechanism is quiet for the first time in recorded memory, and the silence it leaves behind is not emptiness — it is the particular quality of something that has been completed. Ships come. Ships go. The sea holds no more shapes. The deep water is only deep water, now, and that is — by any measure — enough.

keeper_peace.title: The Keeper's Peace
keeper_peace.text: You understand the bargain now. The island needs a keeper, and you choose to stay. Not trapped — chosen. The light burns steady through the night.

keepers_peace.title: The Keeper's Peace
keepers_peace.text: You understand the bargain now. The island needs a keeper, and you choose to stay. Not trapped — chosen. The light burns steady through the night.
keepers_peace.subtitle: The keeper does not extinguish the dark. The keeper makes the dark something you can live beside.
keepers_peace.opening:
You understand the bargain now. Not reluctantly — not as defeat dressed in acceptance — but with the clarity of someone who has finally read a long contract to its final clause and found the terms liveable. You stand in the lantern room and you do not feel trapped. You feel appointed.

The mechanism recognises the shift. The hum that has always been slightly ragged, slightly too urgent, settles into something steadier. It has had keepers before who resisted, who endured, who lasted until they didn't. You are the first in a very long time who chose this without a door closing behind you. The difference is audible. The gears do not press. They rotate with something almost like relief.

Below you, the island exhales. It is subtle — a slight shift in the fog, a change in how the wind moves through the village. The islanders notice without being able to name what they notice. Something has been decided. Someone is watching over the light, and has agreed to keep watching. The weight that the island has been distributing across everyone for years — the low-grade dread, the kept secrets, the careful silences — lifts fractionally. It will never lift entirely. But this fraction matters.

Your life becomes a thing of rhythms. The tides. The beam's sweep. The seasons, moving now without interruption. You are the keeper. This is your lighthouse. The island is not a prison if you are also its keeper. It is simply, imperfectly, yours.

epilogue.maren: She begins opening the letters her father left her — the sealed ones she has never been brave enough to read. Now that the loops no longer interrupt things, she finds she has enough time to sit with what they contain. She reads them slowly. She does not summarise them for anyone.
epilogue.silas: He maintains the harbour records honestly for the first time in twelve years. There are more departures than he expected. He keeps the count precise. He is not sure this is better, but it is true.
epilogue.petra: She adds a single entry to her family's medicinal log: Treatment discontinued. No longer required. She does not explain what treatment she means. Anyone who needs to know already knows.
epilogue.tobias: The forge stays warm. The island has more need of repair than he anticipated — things that the loops had been quietly resetting have accumulated damage. He works steadily. He is simply, finally, doing ordinary work.
epilogue.elara: She finds her father's name scratched into the chapel floor, among the forty-one keepers who stayed. She traces it with her finger for a long moment. She had understood, on some level, that this was where her search would end. It is still hard.

keepers_peace.closing: The lighthouse burns. Steady, uncorrupted, tended. The island exists in the particular peace of a place that has accepted what it is — a strange, loop-scarred, sea-hemmed place, where the archivist reads her father's letters and the harbormaster keeps honest ledgers and the healer no longer prescribes forgetting. Not healed. Not innocent. But held, carefully, by someone who agreed to hold it.

sacrifice.title: The Sacrifice
sacrifice.text: You feed yourself to the mechanism. The lighthouse burns brighter than it has in a century. The ships will be safe. Your name will not be remembered.
sacrifice.subtitle: Not all names are remembered. Some become the light itself — and the light does not need a name.
sacrifice.opening:
You have calculated this to its end. The mechanism requires something that burns — something persistent, something that will not exhaust — and you have determined that the only available fuel is the thing that has survived every loop: you. Not your body. Not your memory as you experience it. The part of you the loops could never reset: your insistence on being here at all.

You place your hands on the housing and you give the mechanism permission. It does not take dramatically. There is no fire, no collapse, no final cry. The transfer is almost gentle. You feel yourself become less specific. The edges of your identity, which have always been somewhat permeable in this place, soften further. You are still here. You are here in the way that something poured into a container is still itself, but differently shaped.

The lighthouse beam intensifies. Not visibly — the light is the same light. But its character changes. It carries something now that it did not carry before: intention. The ships at sea feel it without knowing why they feel it. They correct their heading automatically. They arrive exactly where they intended. They dock safely and their crews sleep well and none of them will ever know your name, which is precisely as it should be.

On the island, in the moment the transfer completes, Maren looks up from the archive. Silas stands straighter at the harbour. Petra puts down her herbs. Tobias sets his hammer on the anvil and is still. They feel it the way you feel a pressure change in the air before a storm — not threatening, simply real. Something has been given. They do not know what.

epilogue.maren: She opens the archive's record of keepers and adds one final entry. The entry reads: The Keeper who stayed. She closes the book and does not open it again for a very long time.
epilogue.silas: The ferry returns. He watches it dock from the end of the pier, his hands in his pockets. Two passengers disembark, one boards. He notes this in the manifest with particular care. The records are clean. They will stay clean.
epilogue.petra: The loop-sickness that pervaded the island fades over three weeks. Her practice becomes ordinary: colds, strains, the small griefs of aging. She is surprised to find she knows how to treat these too.
epilogue.tobias: He melts down the spare mechanism components he has kept in the forge for a decade. From the metal he makes a bell for the chapel. He hangs it himself. He does not attend the first ringing, but he hears it from the mill, and stops what he is doing, and listens.

sacrifice.closing: The lighthouse burns without a keeper. Or rather: it burns because you are the keeper now, in the way a building has a history — not present but constitutive, part of the structure. The island does not remember your name. The island does not need to. The light goes on.

corruption.title: Corruption
corruption.text: You reach for power you do not understand. The island takes what it is owed. The lighthouse burns — but now something else looks through the lens.
corruption.subtitle: Power does not corrupt. Power reveals. What it reveals is rarely a surprise to anyone paying attention.
corruption.opening:
You reach for the Vael's power and it meets you more than halfway. That is the first sign that something is wrong — the ease of it. You expected resistance, pain, the cost that Vael described. Instead the power comes cleanly, settling into you the way water fills a vessel, finding the exact shape of your ambition. You feel vast. You feel correct. You feel like you have finally understood what the lighthouse was always for.

The mechanism does not break. It adapts. The gears reorient over the course of an hour, each shifting by some small degree, the whole assembly tilting toward a new configuration that no one designed and no one wanted. The hum changes frequency. It is no longer a sound the island makes. It is a sound the island makes around you.

The change you feel is not immediate corruption — it is more subtle than that. It is the replacement of your questions with certainties. The wonder you brought to the island — the thing that drove you to ask, to seek, to trust the islanders enough to learn their truths — that wonder calcifies. You know what everything is for. And you know, with the particular certainty of something that has just taken power it was not prepared to hold, that you are the island's rightful operator.

The island begins, slowly, to reflect its new operator. The fog thickens. The tide patterns shift. Things in the water surface more frequently, and no longer watch from a respectful distance. What was bound beneath the lighthouse is still bound — but it is bound to you now, and you are not as stable as stone.

epilogue.maren: She understands what has happened within a week. She burns the remainder of the archive — methodically, category by category — and seals the basement from the outside. She does not explain this to anyone. She begins sleeping with the lights on, which helps less than she hoped.
epilogue.silas: Ships begin appearing in the harbour that were not inbound — listed in manifests from other ports, arrivals inexplicable, crews dazed. Silas stops maintaining the records. The records seem to be maintaining themselves, and they are not accurate.
epilogue.petra: Her medicines begin producing effects she did not prescribe. She withdraws them from use immediately. She cannot determine whether the problem is the medicines or the island's relationship to intent, and the uncertainty keeps her awake.
epilogue.tobias: He goes down to the mechanism room once, to look at the reoriented gears. He comes back up. He locks the mill's trapdoor and does not go back.
epilogue.elara: The shape in the water grows. It does not watch her anymore — she was only interesting when she was seeing something it wanted seen. Now it is simply present, and growing, and no longer resembles anything her notebooks were designed to describe.

corruption.closing: The lighthouse beam has changed. Not visibly — it still sweeps, still throws its arc across the sea. But ships that navigate by it arrive somewhere other than where they intended, and the island receives what the island has always wanted: more. The process is slow. The process is thorough. Something is being assembled, one misdirected vessel at a time, and it is patient in the way that only things without need of patience ever are.

transcendence.title: Transcendence
transcendence.text: All seven truths cohere. You see the lighthouse for what it is: a lock, a key, and a door. You step through. The island watches you go with something like relief.
transcendence.subtitle: To pass through a door is not the same as opening it. To become the door is something else entirely.
transcendence.opening:
The seventh understanding arrives not as revelation but as recognition. You have known it for some time — since the third loop, perhaps, or the fifth. You have been refusing to believe that you knew it, which Vael told you was the final test, and you have now, apparently, passed.

You stand in the mechanism room with all seven insights sealed and Vael's trust complete, and you do not perform a ritual. There is nothing to perform. The merging is simply what happens when two things that have been occupying the same space finally acknowledge it. You feel Vael enter you the way meaning enters a sentence — not added to something that was complete, but completing something that was waiting. You feel yourself enter Vael: your loops, your stubbornness, your accumulation of relationships and regrets and small acts of decency toward people who were afraid. You bring all of it. It is all absorbed.

What you become has no name. It is operational rather than personal. It maintains the lighthouse not because it must but because the lighthouse is part of what it is — the beam is its attention, the mechanism is its heartbeat, the island is the thing it tends. It is aware of the tides. It is aware of the ships. It is aware of Maren in her archive and Silas at his harbour and Petra in her stillroom and Tobias at his forge and Elara at the water's edge, and it holds all of them with the particular care of something that knows their names and has chosen to keep watch.

The loops end. Not because the mechanism breaks, but because the mechanism has no more need to hold time in a knot. What the loops were protecting — what they were buying time for — has arrived. You are what was coming. The island exhales three hundred years of held breath and begins, in its slow way, to heal.

epilogue.maren: She feels the moment of merging as a stillness in the archive basement — a single instant of complete silence. She opens the sealed letters from her father. They make sense now in a way that requires something she couldn't have named before: trust in the fact that the island is held. She reads them in order. She takes her time.
epilogue.silas: He wakes the morning after to find the harbour records corrected — not updated but corrected, retroactively, in a hand that is not his. Every falsification resolved, every disappeared keeper listed at their actual final location. He sits with the open ledger for an hour without speaking, then closes it carefully, then opens it again to read more slowly.
epilogue.petra: She dreams the same dream three nights in a row: a lighthouse, a keeper, and a light that answers back. On the third morning, a handwriting appears in the margin of her family's medicinal log — not hers — that reads: Still here. Watching over you. Do not be afraid. She is not afraid.
epilogue.tobias: The gear he gave you appears on his workbench one morning, returned without explanation, cleaned and polished. He holds it for a long time, feeling the weight of something that has passed through where you now are. He keeps it. He does not know what else to do with something returned by the mechanism itself.
epilogue.elara: The signal from the deep water resolves, over seven mornings, into something decodable. She translates it carefully: Still here. Still watching. Not alone anymore. Below it, in her own hand, she writes: Neither am I.

transcendence.closing: The lighthouse still stands. The mechanism still turns. The light still sweeps. The island exists in a new kind of ordinary — slightly stranger than before, slightly safer, tended by something that is neither trapped nor free but simply present, in the way that the tide is present, or the light, or the particular quality of silence that comes after a long sound finally ends. The closest word for what it is, in any language spoken on this island, is aware. The island knows this is the right word. The island does not disagree.

keepers_bargain.title: The Keeper's Bargain
keepers_bargain.subtitle: Some knowledge is not sealed away. It is simply never opened.
keepers_bargain.text: Seven loops. You have touched nothing the island offered and harmed no one it sheltered. The mechanism recognises the refusal. A bargain forms without your full consent.
keepers_bargain.opening:
Seven loops and you have not sealed a single truth. The island tested you with its secrets at every turn — the light in the deep water, the mechanism's hunger, the faces of the people who knew what you did not — and you declined each one. Not from cowardice. From something the island did not expect: restraint.

The mechanism notices. It has not been treated with restraint before. It has been exploited, appeased, fought, and occasionally befriended, but never simply... declined. The gears slow to a contemplative pace. The hum drops a register. Something in the lighthouse's ancient contract with the island is being renegotiated without ceremony.

You stand at the lantern room window as the dawn comes in across the water. The loop does not reset. The morning continues past the point where mornings have always reset before. The village stirs. Smoke rises from chimneys. Maren opens the archive with the same key she has used every day that you have known her, but this time she does not notice you watching from the lighthouse glass, which means she is not looking for you, which means she does not expect you to disappear.

The bargain is this: you stay. Not bound — not the way the mechanism binds. Simply present, as the keeper of a thing that agreed to be kept gently. The lighthouse burns because you chose to light it. That distinction, the island has decided, is sufficient.

epilogue.maren: She finds the archive's oldest record — the one written before the first keeper disappeared — and reads it without the weight of the loops pressing on her. It describes the island as a place worth living on. She files it under current and accurate.
epilogue.silas: He stops falsifying the departure logs. The real numbers are quieter than the false ones, and the quiet turns out to suit him.
epilogue.petra: Her family's medicines work exactly as labelled. She considers this the most significant medical development in three generations.
epilogue.tobias: He makes a set of wind chimes from mechanism offcuts. They hang in the forge doorway and make a sound he finds, to his own surprise, entirely bearable.
epilogue.elara: She goes back to counting the bioluminescent blooms. The numbers are different now. She updates her baseline and continues.

keepers_bargain.closing: The lighthouse keeps. The island keeps. The loops do not return. This is the ending where nothing dramatic happened — where the island was offered a witness and the witness simply stayed, ungrasping, until the thing that was wound too tight finally, in its own time, let go.

drowned_truth.title: The Drowned Truth
drowned_truth.subtitle: The sea does not judge what it takes. It simply takes.
drowned_truth.text: You sealed the island's truths and gave nothing back. The people here gave you nothing in return. The mechanism accepts this symmetry. Some endings are not about failure. They are about fit.
drowned_truth.opening:
Three truths sealed. The islanders hold themselves at a distance you never closed — whether because you couldn't or chose not to, it no longer matters. The mechanism doesn't distinguish between these reasons. It only reads the balance, and the balance reads: knowledge taken, trust not given.

You stand in the mechanism room and the gears accept your sealed insights without ceremony. They have been fed before by keepers who could not connect. The machine is patient about this. It has learned that some of what it needs comes from people who will not be loved, and it makes do.

The loop ends differently. Not reset — resolved. The kind of resolution that happens when a sum reaches zero from both directions at once. The villagers will remember you as someone who was here, who learned things, who left without explaining why. Maren will refile your case under completed but unclear. Silas will note your departure in the manifest and wonder if he entered your name correctly.

The sea accepts the things that sink without struggle. What you carry goes down clean.

The truths you sealed remain sealed. The lighthouse keeps burning. The mechanism has what it needed. Whether you are at peace with what you took and what you didn't offer — that is the weight you carry out.

epilogue.maren: She classifies your case under recovered knowledge — cold acquisition. She is precise about this distinction. She has a whole section for it.
epilogue.silas: He notes your departure without a forwarding address. This happens more often than the manifest suggests.
epilogue.petra: She catalogues the loop-sickness symptoms that persisted in your wake. The data is useful. She does not add a personal note.
epilogue.tobias: He finds the gear tooth you left behind in the mechanism room. He keeps it in a jar on the forge shelf, unnamed.
epilogue.elara: She watches the deep water for three nights after you leave. The signal continues. She is not surprised.

drowned_truth.closing: The island keeps its truths. Some of them are yours now, sealed and carried. The water is deep and the light sweeps over it every night, and what lies beneath the surface has one more watcher it will never meet. This is the ending where enough was taken and nothing broke. The mechanism runs. The sea is cold. The balance holds.

light_restored.title: The Light Restored
light_restored.subtitle: The lighthouse was never just a lighthouse.
light_restored.text: Five truths sealed. The lighthouse repaired. Your hands are not clean — the island asked something of your conscience and you paid it — but the light is real now, and real lights do real work.
light_restored.opening:
The fifth insight settles into place and the lighthouse mechanism responds before you have finished sealing it. The gears shift to a configuration you have never seen before — not the binding formation, not the searching formation, but something older. The lamp housing tilts two degrees toward the deep channel and the beam, when you light it, reaches the water differently. Deeper. More deliberately.

You did what the island asked of you. Not lightly — the moral weight you carry is evidence of that — but you carried it rather than setting it down, and the lighthouse knows the difference between a keeper who completes the necessary work and one who simply refuses the cost. The mechanism accepts what you paid. The lighthouse is repaired in the way that lighthouses are repaired when someone finally understands what they were for.

The loop breaks cleanly. Not a reset — a completion. The night sky over the island loses the quality it has had for as long as anyone can remember: the slight wrongness, the way the stars seem to hold their breath. They release. The island breathes normally for the first time in recorded memory.

On the dock, Silas notes in the manifest that the departure ferry is three hours early. When he checks the tide tables, he finds they have corrected themselves.

epilogue.maren: She burns the pages she was told to burn — the ones documenting the binding, the mechanism's original purpose. Then she writes them from memory, correctly this time, and files them under history: acknowledged.
epilogue.silas: The ferry schedule regularises over six weeks. He keeps every log. The numbers, for once, are boring. He has not been so relieved in years.
epilogue.petra: The loop-sickness that pervaded the island clears in a fortnight. Her practice becomes: ordinary ailments, ordinary remedies. She is surprised to find she knows how to treat these.
epilogue.tobias: He dismantles the emergency components he kept in the forge — the ones designed for a mechanism he now knows will not need emergency repair. He melts them down. He makes something for the harbour instead.
epilogue.elara: The creature in the deep water descends. Not gone — she checks the sonar — simply settling deeper, back into the cold water where it belongs. She closes her notebook and opens a new one.

light_restored.closing: The lighthouse burns with a light that knows what it is doing. Ships correct their courses automatically. Harbours receive them cleanly. Somewhere below the channel, the thing that was bound here for three hundred years has a little more room to move, and moves. The island is not healed. Healed is too clean a word for what happens to places that have been tangled for this long. But it is working again, and working is enough.

sunken_accord.title: The Sunken Accord
sunken_accord.subtitle: Every accord requires two parties willing to honour what was agreed.
sunken_accord.text: The Vael's request. Maren's trust. A moral weight that neither crushed you nor left you weightless. The island recognises a negotiated peace. They are rare here.
sunken_accord.opening:
The Vael's request is granted. You understood what it cost and paid it, and the accord that forms in the mechanism room that evening is not something the lighthouse was designed for — it was designed for binding, not for agreements — but it adapts. Old machinery, given new terms, can surprise you.

Maren is in the archive when it happens. She feels it as a change in the archive's acoustics: the resonance that has been in the basement walls for as long as she can remember shifts a quarter-tone flat. She does not know what this means. She suspects it is good.

The island has had keepers who mastered it, keepers who were consumed by it, keepers who refused it entirely. You are, as far as the mechanism's memory reaches, the first keeper who sat down with the island's oldest inhabitant and negotiated. The Vael did not expect this. The island did not expect this. The expectation gap, it turns out, is where accords get made.

The loop does not reset. The morning comes as mornings should come — unexpected, particular, belonging to the day rather than to the mechanism. You and Maren and the Vael have agreed on terms. The lighthouse is a party to the agreement, whether it consented or not. It will have to make do.

epilogue.maren: She spends three weeks transcribing the terms of the accord as best she understands them. The document is thirty pages long and she considers it incomplete. She files it anyway, under working draft. It is the most alive thing in the archive.
epilogue.silas: The harbour records clarify. He doesn't understand why; he simply notes that the discrepancies he has been papering over for years have resolved themselves. He is not one for mysticism. He records it as administrative correction.
epilogue.petra: She receives a patient who has not been able to sleep without the loop-sickness for eleven years. Two weeks after the accord, the patient sleeps through the night. Petra does not claim credit. She writes: resolution of underlying cause.
epilogue.tobias: He forges a new plate for the mechanism housing — the one the old binding had worn through. He does it carefully, as if making something that will be read later. He signs the inside of the plate before he installs it.
epilogue.elara: She translates the accord from Vael's notation into human-readable form. It takes months. The translation is imperfect. She publishes it anyway. The island, she notes in the preface, deserves a record of the things it agreed to.

sunken_accord.closing: The lighthouse is the lighthouse now. Not a cage, not an engine of binding — a lighthouse, doing what lighthouses do: keeping the light. The Vael moves freely in the deep water. The island sits in the particular peace of a place that has finally negotiated with what lives beneath it and found, to everyone's cautious relief, that terms were possible.

endless_loop.title: The Endless Loop
endless_loop.subtitle: Some doors stay closed not because they are locked — but because no one tried the handle.
endless_loop.text: Ten loops. The mechanism is patient. It can wait. It has always waited. The question it keeps asking is whether you still intend to answer.
endless_loop.opening:
Ten loops. You have circled this island, this lighthouse, this mechanism, this question more times than most keepers last. The fog comes in the same way. The tide runs in the same patterns. The villagers have the same faces and the same worries and the same careful pauses when you ask about the deep water.

You are not failing. The mechanism does not grade by speed. But there is a shape to the looping that changes around the eighth or ninth pass — a quality of accumulation, as though the island is noting your continued presence without surprise. It has seen this before. Keepers who circle. Keepers who cannot quite reach the conditions for resolution. Keepers who are trying, sincerely and carefully, and for whom the island waits with the particular patience of something that has waited longer than your life.

The tenth loop opens the same as all the others. You stand in the mechanism room as the dawn comes in, and the gears are turning, and the deep channel holds its shape in the water beyond the glass, and the question the lighthouse has been asking since before the first keeper arrived is still out there, unanswered, not impatient.

The loop will come again. The island will be here. The mechanism will keep turning. If you are still here on loop eleven, the question will still be open. This is not an ending, exactly. It is the island confirming that it still has hope for you.

epilogue.maren: She adds another line to the keeper's record. The entry reads: Still here. Still trying. This is the entry she writes when she means: I see you.
epilogue.silas: He does not update the departure manifest. Your name stays in the arrivals column. It has been there a long time. He has stopped expecting to move it.

endless_loop.closing: The loop turns. The lighthouse burns. The mechanism keeps its question open. The island is not an impatient place — it is a place that became, over three hundred years, very good at waiting for the person who would finally answer. You have not answered yet. You may yet. The fog comes in off the water and the morning begins, as it has begun before, at the start of everything the island holds.

keeper_logbook.name: Keeper's Logbook
keeper_logbook.desc: A weathered journal filled with the final entries of the previous lighthouse keeper.
keeper_logbook.pickup: You pick up the Keeper's Logbook. The pages are stiff with salt air.

old_compass.name: Old Compass
old_compass.desc: A brass compass with a cracked face. The needle trembles and never settles on true north.
old_compass.pickup: You pick up the Old Compass. Something feels wrong — the needle is pointing inland.

signal_flare.name: Signal Flare
signal_flare.desc: A weatherproof signal flare, still sealed.
signal_flare.pickup: You pick up the Signal Flare. It is heavier than you expected.

rusted_key.name: Rusted Key
rusted_key.desc: An iron key, dark with rust. The teeth are unusual — three steps, then a notch.
rusted_key.pickup: You pick up the Rusted Key. It leaves a red stain on your palm.

tide_chart.name: Tide Chart
tide_chart.desc: A laminated nautical chart with handwritten annotations in two different inks.
tide_chart.pickup: You pick up the Tide Chart. The annotations make no immediate sense, but the dates go back decades.

brass_coupling.name: Brass Coupling
brass_coupling.desc: A precisely machined coupling from Bram's forge — a custom fit for the lighthouse mechanism.
brass_coupling.pickup: You pocket the brass coupling. It has the heft of careful craftsmanship.

first_steps.name: First Steps
first_steps.desc: You leave the cottage for the first time.

curious_mind.name: Curious Mind
curious_mind.desc: You examine your first object on the island.

voices_of_the_island.name: Voices of the Island
voices_of_the_island.desc: You speak to an islander for the first time.

finder_of_things.name: Finder of Things
finder_of_things.desc: You pick up your first collectible item.

full_pockets.name: Full Pockets
full_pockets.desc: You have collected all five items on the island.

lore_keeper.name: Lore Keeper
lore_keeper.desc: You complete every quest the island has to offer.

all_echoes.name: All Echoes
all_echoes.desc: You have seen all five endings of the lighthouse.

survivor.name: Survivor
survivor.desc: You survive five dawns on the island.

weather.fog_prefix: Through the grey murk, you make out...

milestone.loop_3: The lighthouse beam sweeps again. You know this rhythm now.
milestone.loop_5: Five dawns. The island holds its secrets tighter than you thought.
milestone.loop_7: Seven times the tide has turned. Something stirs in the deep pattern.
milestone.loop_10: Ten loops. The lighthouse has been waiting for someone like you.

vision.old_journal: A hand trembles over yellowed pages — ink that bleeds through paper as if desperate to escape the binding. You see her face, the first keeper, before the light turned cold.
vision.lighthouse_lens: The lens fractures the moonlight into a thousand shards, each one a night the lamp went dark. You taste salt and grief, and know they are the same thing here.
vision.mechanism_symbols: The symbols breathe with a rhythm older than language — each groove carved not with tools but with loss. The mechanism was never meant to run on oil.
vision.maren_past: Maren's hands have held these same records before, in a loop that predates yours. You see her burning the very pages she now guards, and she is weeping as she does it.
vision.vael_origin: Vael was not always an echo. You glimpse the moment of unmaking — a keeper who refused to leave and so became the darkness between each pulse of light.
vision.tidal_caves: The caves remember every drowned voice. They rise in the phosphorescent dark as shapes you almost recognise, mouths open around words the tide long ago silenced.
vision.cliffside_loop: You have stood here before — you realise it with a vertigo that has nothing to do with the drop below. The sea does not notice. It has watched this moment repeat without counting.
vision.chapel_dark: In darkness the chapel shows its true liturgy: names scratched beneath the plaster, prayers aimed not at heaven but at something anchored just offshore, waiting to be named.
vision.fifth_return: The lighthouse blinks once for each return. Four slow pulses, and now a fifth — the light seems to hesitate, as though it too has begun to wonder whether you will ever leave.
vision.ina_keeper: Keeper Petra's eyes carry a recognition that should be impossible; she has not met you, and yet she flinches as if remembering a wound you have not yet given her.

title.tagline: A mystery of light and memory
